data.content_ref: inline:The claim defines the strict-advantage regime as requiring the critic to output calibrated confidence and abstain on right-majority sets, but the experiment must use the SAME pool of candidates as self-consistency and the SAME critic calls. Introducing a tunable threshold tau over a continuous confidence score fundamentally alters the critic's decision policy compared to the measured binary-veto arms (precision 0.125 / recall 1.0), so the proposed experiment no longer tests harness-style criticism as defined by the problem — it tests a different, thresholded abstention mechanism. The problem asks when the adversarial critic filtering the candidate pool beats unfiltered majority vote, not when a redesigned confidence-thresholded critic does.
